title: Association of mortality and combined oxidative capacity of ozone and nitrogen dioxide
authors: Micheline de Sousa Zanotti Stagliorio Coelho
Susana das Neves Pereira da Silva
data_sources: MCC Multi-Country Multi-City Collaborative Research Network
National and regional air-quality monitoring stations (per-country)

data_scope.exposure_metric: Ox_wt (O3 and NO2 weighted by standard electrode potential)

core_conclusion: The weighted oxidative capacity index Ox_wt (average of O3 and NO2 concentrations weighted by standard electrode potential) associates more strongly with mortality than either pollutant alone: a 10-ppb increase over 2 days links to +0.82% total, +1.09% cardiovascular, and +0.88% respiratory deaths across 380 cities in 23 countries/regions (1985-2020). Ox_wt attributable deaths exceed those from O3 or NO2 individually but are fewer than their sum, confirming partial non-additivity and supporting Ox_wt as a parsimonious public health indicator.
why_it_matters: First global multi-country time-series analysis of a single mechanistically motivated composite gaseous oxidative index; resolves confounded double-counting of O3 and NO2; reveals regional and temporal heterogeneity guiding regulatory prioritization; directly supports replacing dual O3/NO2 standards with a composite threshold; reproducible code archived openly.
why_nature: Planetary-scale evidence base (380 cities, 23 countries, 35 years); novel composite indicator grounded in electrochemical mechanism rather than statistical convenience; direct link between urban air quality governance and health sustainability; geographic heterogeneity addresses environmental justice across income contexts.
added_value_to_my_research: Satellite-derived NO2/O3 surfaces fused with ground monitors via spatiotemporal kernel density estimation to replace city-average Ox_wt with intra-urban gridded exposures for MGWR/GWR health models
NDVI greenness as spatial effect modifier of Ox_wt-mortality slope — links healthy-city urban analytics to environmental epidemiology via GeoAI
SHAP-attributed XGBoost model predicting city-level Ox_wt effect heterogeneity from urban/socioeconomic features — explainable ML for multi-city health exposure studies
Unified ROS index combining Ox_wt with PM2.5 oxidative potential via multi-source data fusion (satellite AOD + monitors + chemical transport models)
2SFCA/R2SFCA healthcare accessibility as effect modifier of Ox_wt-mortality — integrates health geography accessibility work directly into environmental epidemiology

related.title: Nexus between residential air pollution and physiological stress is moderated by greenness
related.relation: greenness as spatial effect modifier of air-pollution health impacts — natural extension to modifying Ox_wt-mortality associations

related.title: Air pollution below US regulatory standards and cardiovascular diseases using a double negative control approach
related.relation: causal inference approach for air-pollution cardiovascular mortality at sub-threshold levels — methodological complement to attributable-fraction analysis

related.title: Global air pollution exposure and poverty
related.relation: global scale linkage of air pollution burden to socioeconomic deprivation — supports equity extension of Ox_wt attributable deaths

related.title: Air pollution control strategies directly limiting national health damages in the US
related.relation: policy translation of pollution-mortality evidence to health-damage-limiting control strategies — downstream application of composite indicators like Ox_wt

figures.caption: Associations between Ox_wt and daily total mortality — pooled and region-stratified effect estimates across 380 cities
Lag patterns for the associations between Ox_wt and daily mortality — temporal dynamics of short-term effects
Concentration-response relationships between Ox_wt and daily mortality — non-linear exposure-risk curves
